16. **`src/app/pages/auth/helpers/idle-timeout.service.ts:36`** — `setIdleTimeForUser` dereferences `user.user_id` without a null check; `getUserFromToken()` returns `null` for a missing/corrupt token, which would throw inside the guard chain. **Possible** (guard normally ensures a token exists first).

17. **Unguarded Vex demo routes** — `apps/chat`, `apps/mail`, `apps/social`, `apps/contacts`, `apps/calendar`, `apps/scrumboard`, `apps/aio-table`, `ui/*`, `documentation` (app-routing.module.ts:228-330) have no `canActivate` at all and ship demo pages with fake data into the production bundle. **Certain.**

AGENTS.md §11 documents the equation split as intentional, but the drift across the editor/picker copies is an active bug factory. **Certain.**

19. **God objects** — `rfq.component.ts` is 4,066 lines (grid config, valueSetters, view persistence, dialogs, clipboard, export, role logic in one class); `main.service.ts` is ~150 untyped endpoints returning `any`, and every method manually attaches auth headers that `UserAuthInterceptor` already adds globally (redundant double mechanism). **Certain.**

20. **`src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts:171-181`** — seven `window['…']` globals set in the constructor (`equation`, `dialog`, `_snackBar`, `router`, `itemService`, …), with 38 `window[...]` accesses across the app (`rfq-copy-guard.ts:12-13` even uses them as DI defaults); documented as a legacy idiosyncrasy in AGENTS.md and banned for new code, but still fragile (breaks if RFQ page wasn't visited first). **Certain.**

21. **`src/app/pages/comments/comments/comments.component.html:10-13`** — `getRootComments()` and `getReplies(comment.id)` are called in the template, re-filtering arrays on every change-detection cycle, and `track comment` tracks freshly created objects so the whole list re-renders on each reload. **Certain.**

22. **`src/app/app.module.ts`** — `AppRoutingModule` and `MatOptionModule` are each imported twice in the imports array; `{ provide: JWT_OPTIONS, useValue: JWT_OPTIONS }` provides the injection token as its own value (works only because nothing reads real options). **Certain.**

23. **`src/app/pages/auth/helpers/auth.service.ts:39-44,68`** — `setUserAuth(token)` ignores its parameter and re-reads localStorage; `getUserFromToken(): Promise<boolean>` actually returns a decoded payload object or null (wrong type, and callers cast around it). **Certain.**

24. **`src/app/app-routing.module.ts:315-318`** — the `pages/error-401` route loads `Error500Module` (which in fact renders 401 content); there is no real 500 error page and the module/file naming is inverted. **Certain.**

25. **`src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts:381-395`** — `uploadThumbnailImage` uses raw `fetch` with a fake `responseType: 'blob'` option suppressed by `@ts-ignore` (fetch has no such option); misleading and bypasses all interceptors/error handling. **Certain.**

26. **`src/app/helpers/services/helper.service.ts:54-77`** — `resizeImage` never rejects and `image.onload` never fires for non-image input, so the promise hangs forever; the no-itemId path in file-upload-dialog generates a "thumbnail" even for PDF content, which would hit exactly this hang (spinner stuck). It also force-squashes images to 200x200 ignoring aspect ratio. **Possible** (depends on a PDF reaching that path).

27. **216 remaining `.toPromise()` call sites** across ~45 files (deprecated RxJS API, removed in RxJS 8) despite the stated standard. **Certain.**

## Documentation problems

28. **`README.md:58` and `docs/development.md:148`** — claim "all async operations use `firstValueFrom()` (not `.toPromise()`)"; 216 `.toPromise()` calls remain (AGENTS.md:801 more accurately says only the RFQ module was converted). **Certain.**

29. **`HANDOFF.md` (on-disk, modified)** — states the factory bulk-upload feature is "IMPLEMENTED, NOT YET COMMITTED … all changes unstaged", but `git status` is clean for those files and they are tracked — the feature was committed and the doc is stale; the file also has a numbering bug (section "## 2. Notification trigger points" immediately followed by a duplicate "## 1. Notification trigger points"). **Certain.**

30. **`docs/architecture.md:104,133`** — says password login is `POST /auth/login` (actual endpoint is `POST /findUserLoginInfo`, main.service.ts:113) and that `UserAuthInterceptor` attaches `Authorization: Bearer <token>` (the code sends the raw token with no `Bearer` prefix, user-auth.interceptor.ts:19). Also says `rfq.component.ts` is "~3600 lines" (now 4,066). **Certain.**

# Overall assessment

This is a functioning production app with some genuinely well-built recent code (the API error-message helper, loader interceptor, grid view persistence, SSO wrapper, and the AGENTS.md incident log are all thoughtful), sitting on a legacy foundation with real fragility: the auth layer has a hangs-forever token check, decorative role guards, and an impersonation-token leak; and the codebase carries heavy technical debt in the form of 4-6× duplicated component trees, a 4,000-line RFQ god component, a 150-endpoint untyped API service, and `window[]` globals. The highest-value fixes are small and surgical — an error handler in `verifyToken`, switching role-annotated routes to `RoleGuard`, adding the two missing `ngOnDestroy`s, `catchError` in the notification poll, and clearing `admin_token` on logout — while the duplication and god-component issues are longer-term refactoring debt that the team's own docs already acknowledge.</result>

All six reviews are complete. Here's the combined report — written in plain language, since this is your codebase and the details matter more than the jargon.

## The big picture

Every one of your six repos follows the same pattern: **the newer code is genuinely good** — clean, tested, well-documented — but the **older "core" code carries real problems**. The most serious issues repeat across multiple repos, which actually makes them easier to fix: solve one, you have the recipe for the rest.

I'd group everything into three buckets: **🔴 security (fix this week)**, **🟠 real bugs (things silently not working)**, and **🟡 cleanup (slow, messy, but not breaking)**.

---

## 🔴 Security — the urgent ones

**1. A secret password that lets anyone log in as anyone.** In the backend, there's a hardcoded "master password" (`Sha14171518`) — type it and you're logged in as any user or vendor, no questions asked. This is the single most dangerous thing found. *(designflow-backend)*

**2. Live passwords/API keys sitting in the code.** The same external-system key (`Z21355JALT13A54L9X5` for ColdLion) is copy-pasted into three different repos, plus a live email-service key in the backend. Anyone who can see the code has these keys, and you can't change them without a code change. These need to be **rotated (changed) and moved into secure storage**. *(backend, item-master, tracking, data-syncing)*

**3. Doors with no locks.** Several web addresses (endpoints) that should require a login don't check for one:
- An AI/chat feature anyone can call — meaning **a stranger could run up your AI bill with no limit** (backend).
- A "dump the entire item catalog" address with no login required (item-master).
- The lead-time settings that drive your production date calculations — anyone can change them (tracking).

**4. The website's "admin-only" pages aren't actually locked.** In the frontend, pages marked admin-only (including user management, where impersonation happens) can be opened by any logged-in user, including vendors. The lock was written but never connected. *(frontend)*

---

## 🟠 Real bugs — things silently broken or at risk

The scariest theme here: **failures disguised as success.** Across the backend, data-syncing, item-master, and tracking, the standard error-handling pattern is wrong (`result(null, err)`) — when a database operation *fails*, the system reports **"success"** and sends back garbage. This means broken things can run for a long time without anyone noticing.

Concrete examples of that in action:

- **Data-syncing is substantially broken end-to-end.** Several of the main sync jobs either do nothing at all (item headers, attachments), crash on an undefined variable (vendor/customer updates), or aim at a web address that doesn't exist (image upload) — and every one of those failures is reported as success. Synced data also gets silently blanked out or mis-labeled on every run (e.g. every item is forced to the same division code). *(designflow-data-syncing — the worst-off repo)*

- **Choosing a vendor may never save.** In the backend, the "select vendor" action writes to the wrong database table, so the choice likely doesn't stick. *(backend)*

- **An infinite loop.** In item-master, generating an item number when there's a naming collision can loop forever, hanging the request and hammering the database. *(item-master)*

- **Lead-time changes don't take effect until a restart.** In tracking, the production date calculations are loaded once at startup and never refreshed — so when someone updates a lead-time template, the change is ignored until the service restarts. *(tracking)*

- **Login can hang forever.** In the frontend, if the token-check request fails (network blip, server error), the app gets stuck on a blank screen instead of bouncing you to the login page. *(frontend)*

- **Multi-file uploads quietly drop files.** The upload dialog lets you add several files but only ever uploads the first one — the rest vanish with no warning. And failed uploads still save a broken link. *(frontend)*

- **Two pages leak memory.** The RFQ and Manage Users pages don't clean up after themselves, so the app gets slower the more you use them. (Interestingly, the sibling pages do this correctly — it's copy-paste drift.) *(frontend)*

- **A few "mark as read" / comment features let one user act on another user's data** (privacy leaks) — e.g. a factory user can read another factory's samples and prices by guessing IDs. *(backend, tracking)*

---

## 🟡 Cleanup — slow, messy, but not breaking

- **Slow database habits.** Many screens load *entire* tables into memory and filter afterward, or run one query per row in a loop (50 items = 100 queries). As your data grows, these get noticeably slower. Full SQL debug-logging is also left switched on in production across most repos, which bloats logs and slows things down. *(all backend repos)*

- **Heavy copy-paste.** The frontend has the same components duplicated 4–6 times with small differences (one core file, `rfq.component.ts`, is 4,000+ lines doing everything). When you fix a bug in one copy, the others keep it. This is the main reason small bugs spread. *(frontend especially)*

- **Test suites that test nothing.** Several repos have a "tests must pass before deploy" rule, but the only test is literally checking that 1+1=2. So that safety gate is giving you false confidence. *(bff, data-syncing, others)*

- **Dead code and broken docs.** Lots of unused files (some that would crash if anyone called them), and documentation that contradicts the code — wrong web addresses, stale file paths pointing at `D:\repos\dflow` (the old location), and claims like "all uploads are validated" that aren't true.

---

## My recommendation on order of attack

1. **This week:** Remove the master password, rotate + hide the API keys, and add login checks to the AI endpoint (the bill-runaway risk) and the other unprotected routes.
2. **Next:** Fix the "errors reported as success" pattern — it's the same fix repeated, and it's *hiding* all the other bugs.
3. **Then:** The data-syncing repo deserves dedicated attention — multiple core jobs are simply not working.
4. **Ongoing:** The duplication and giant-file cleanup is real debt, but it's not on fire — tackle it gradually.

Want me to start on any of these? The master-password removal and the API-key cleanup are quick, surgical, and the highest-value — I'd suggest starting there. I'd do it on a branch and walk you through each change before anything gets committed.

One note: since these are confirmed security issues in your live code, I'd recommend we handle the fixes ourselves rather than putting the specifics in any shared doc or ticket until they're patched.

# dflow PLM — Full Codebase Audit
*Reviewed: 2026-06-10 | Repos: designflow-backend, designflow-bff, designflow-data-syncing, designflow-frontend, designflow-item-master, designflow-tracking*

---

## How to read this file

Each finding has:
- **Repo** — which of the 6 repos it lives in
- **File + line** — exactly where to find it
- **Severity** — 🔴 High (security or real bug) / 🟠 Medium (likely problem or major inefficiency) / 🟡 Low (cleanup, dead code, style)
- **Confidence** — Certain / Likely / Possible
- **What it is** — plain description
- **What it causes** — real-world consequence

Findings are grouped first by severity, then by repo.

---

## Table of Contents

1. [🔴 High Severity — Security](#-high-severity--security)
2. [🔴 High Severity — Real Bugs](#-high-severity--real-bugs)
3. [🟠 Medium Severity](#-medium-severity)
4. [🟡 Low Severity](#-low-severity)
5. [Documentation Problems](#documentation-problems)
6. [Summary Table](#summary-table)

---

## 🔴 High Severity — Security

---

### BACKEND-H1 — Hardcoded master/backdoor password
- **Repo:** designflow-backend
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:1136`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `makeLoginToken` accepts any account's login if `req.passw == 'Sha14171518'`. This is a universal backdoor: anyone who knows this string can log in as any user or vendor with no other credentials.
- **What it causes:** Complete authentication bypass. Anyone — a developer, a contractor, a disgruntled employee, someone who reads the repo — can log in as any account including admins. This is the single highest-risk finding in the entire codebase.

---

### BACKEND-H2 — Live Brevo/Sendinblue API key committed in source
- **Repo:** designflow-backend
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:3612`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** A live `xkeysib-419f7a93...` email service API key is hardcoded directly in the source code and committed to the repo.
- **What it causes:** Anyone with repo access can read your email/sending logs, send bulk emails from your account, and run up charges. The key cannot be rotated without a code change and a new deploy.

---

### BACKEND-H3 — Unauthenticated paid AI proxy
- **Repo:** designflow-backend
- **File:** `routes/openrouter.router.js:35, 57`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** Neither `GET /api/openrouter/models` nor `POST /api/openrouter/chat` has any `authRole` guard. These routes are fully public.
- **What it causes:** Anyone who can reach the service can run unlimited AI conversations billed to your OpenRouter/DeepSeek account. No login, no API key, no rate limit on your end. This is an unbounded billing abuse risk.

---

### ITEM-MASTER-H1 — Hardcoded ColdLion ERP API key
- **Repo:** designflow-item-master
- **File:** `services/item_library.service.js:673`, `services/item_detail.service.js:470`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** The ColdLion ERP API key `Z21355JALT13A54L9X5` is hardcoded in plain text in two service files.
- **What it causes:** Anyone with repo access can call your external ERP system directly. The key cannot be changed without a code edit and redeploy. (This same key is also hardcoded in designflow-tracking and designflow-data-syncing — four total locations.)

---

### ITEM-MASTER-H2 — Unauthenticated full item-catalog dump
- **Repo:** designflow-item-master
- **File:** `routes/item_library.router.js:12`, `services/item_library.service.js:234-333`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `GET /lib/getApiAllItems` has no `authRole()` middleware. The service returns the entire item catalog with attachments and production-order details, unpaginated, with no login required.
- **What it causes:** Anyone who can reach the service URL gets your complete item database — no username, no password, no token needed.

---

### TRACKING-H1 — Hardcoded ColdLion API key (third copy)
- **Repo:** designflow-tracking
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:541-546`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** The ColdLion API key `Z21355JALT13A54L9X5` and endpoint `http://x5.coldlion.com/...` hardcoded in `syncPrepackAssociations`, over plain HTTP (not HTTPS).
- **What it causes:** Same as ITEM-MASTER-H1. Additionally the plain HTTP means the key travels unencrypted over the wire.

---

### TRACKING-H2 — All lead-time routes have no authentication
- **Repo:** designflow-tracking
- **File:** `routes/lead-time.router.js:6-16`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** Every other router wraps routes in `authRole([...])`. The lead-time router has none. `PUT /lead-time/design-team-times/:id`, `/licensing-times/:id`, and `/factory-times/:id` accept changes from any caller — no login required.
- **What it causes:** Anyone who can reach the service can change the master lead-time templates that drive all production needed-date calculations. Also, the PUTs pass `req.body` straight to `update()` (mass assignment — any field can be overwritten).

---

### DATA-SYNCING-H1 — Hardcoded ColdLion API key (fourth copy)
- **Repo:** designflow-data-syncing
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:497` (and throughout sync helpers)
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** Fourth location of the same hardcoded ColdLion API key.
- **What it causes:** Same as ITEM-MASTER-H1. All four copies need to be rotated simultaneously when remediated.

---

### FRONTEND-H1 — Auth guard hangs forever on network error
- **Repo:** designflow-frontend
- **File:** `src/app/pages/auth/helpers/auth.service.ts:54-66`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `verifyToken()` wraps an HTTP call in a `new Promise` whose `subscribe()` has no error handler. If the `/verifyToken` call fails for any reason (network blip, server 500), the promise never resolves.
- **What it causes:** `AuthGuard.canActivate()` hangs forever. The user gets a permanently blank/frozen screen instead of being redirected to login. Any brief server hiccup locks users out.

---

### FRONTEND-H2 — Role guards are decorative on most "admin-only" pages
- **Repo:** designflow-frontend
- **File:** `src/app/app-routing.module.ts:63-160`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** Most routes are annotated `data: { role: 'admin' }` (todos, itemLibrary, prod-tracking, licensing-tracking, lead-time-calculator, art-piece, archive) but are guarded only by `AuthGuard`, which never reads the role data. Only two routes (`apps/admin/editor`, `apps/factory`) actually use `RoleGuard`.
- **What it causes:** Any logged-in user, including vendors, can open these "admin-only" pages. The restriction exists in code but has no effect.

---

### FRONTEND-H3 — User management page has no role restriction at all
- **Repo:** designflow-frontend
- **File:** `src/app/app-routing.module.ts:166-170`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `/apps/admin/users` — the user management page where impersonation is launched — has only `AuthGuard`, no role restriction whatsoever.
- **What it causes:** Any logged-in user, including vendors, can navigate to the user management page.

---

### FRONTEND-H4 — Impersonation token left in localStorage on logout
- **Repo:** designflow-frontend
- **File:** `src/@vex/layout/toolbar/toolbar-user/toolbar-user-dropdown/toolbar-user-dropdown.component.ts:81-85`, `src/app/helpers/services/impersonation.service.ts:9, 35`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `logout()` removes only `token`. If an admin logs out while impersonating another user, the admin's original JWT remains in localStorage under `admin_token`.
- **What it causes:** A live privileged admin token is left on the machine after logout. On a shared or compromised machine this is a real credential leak. `ImpersonationService` also still reports "impersonating" on next login.

---

## 🔴 High Severity — Real Bugs

---

### BACKEND-H4 — Null-dereference crash on unknown SKU
- **Repo:** designflow-backend
- **File:** `services/autofill.service.js:79-83`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `getPropertyById(itemData.udf_merchgroup06_fk_id)` runs *before* the null check `if (!itemData) return null`. When `getItemBySku` returns null (SKU not found), the code throws `TypeError: Cannot read properties of null`.
- **What it causes:** Any autofill request for an unknown SKU crashes. The autofill routes also have no auth guard, so this is publicly reachable.

---

### BACKEND-H5 — "Select vendor" writes to the wrong database table
- **Repo:** designflow-backend
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:2266-2282`
- **Confidence:** Likely
- **What it is:** `Customer.selectedVendor` calls `sql.GridLayout.update(req.body, { where: { rfqItem_style_number } })`, but `GridLayout` has no `rfqItem_style_number` column. The update targets the wrong table entirely.
- **What it causes:** Choosing a vendor likely never actually saves. The operation either errors silently or updates 0 rows, and the selection is lost.

---

### BACKEND-H6 — `findRFQAddProductLayout` references an undefined variable
- **Repo:** designflow-backend
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:641, 666`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** Inside a loop over `data`, two branches read/write `newData[i]`, but `newData` is never declared anywhere in the function.
- **What it causes:** `ReferenceError` the moment either branch is hit, breaking the Add-Product layout endpoint.

---

### DATA-SYNCING-H1 — Item-header, attachment, and merch-group-header syncs silently write nothing
- **Repo:** designflow-data-syncing
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:61-114`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `findOrCreate()` only has logic for `schemaName === "merchGroup"`. It is called with `'itemHeader'` (line 214), `'itemAttachment'` (line 229), and `'merchGroupHeaders'` (line 637). For those, the function does nothing and returns `[]`.

### DATA-SYNCING-H2 — Image upload calls a route that doesn't exist
- **Repo:** designflow-data-syncing
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:497-517`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `uploadToGCS()` POSTs to `http://localhost:5001/uploadToGCS`, which is this service's own port, and no such route exists in `routes/`. The 404 is swallowed (returns `undefined`), then line 319 calls `publicUrl.replace(...)` on `undefined`.
- **What it causes:** A `TypeError` aborts the entire image sync on every run. No product images are ever uploaded to cloud storage.

---

### DATA-SYNCING-H3 — Vendor and customer update step crashes on an undefined variable
- **Repo:** designflow-data-syncing
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:836-840, 867-871`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** Inside the `.then` of `getExternalVendor`/`getExternalCustomer`, the `where` condition references `data[i]`, but `i` is not in scope → `ReferenceError` on every run.
- **What it causes:** Existing vendor and customer rows are **never updated** — only brand-new records are inserted. The error is returned as an HTTP 200 "success" so it's been going unnoticed.

### DATA-SYNCING-H4 — Duplicate merch-group rows created when DB data is newer
- **Repo:** designflow-data-syncing
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:161-170`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `findOrCreateWithSSE` adds `modTime: { [Op.lte]: modTime }` to the `findOrCreate` where-clause. If the existing DB row has a *newer* `modTime`, the lookup misses and a duplicate row with older data is created. The `merchGroup` table has no unique constraint on the composite key.

### DATA-SYNCING-H5 — Freeform/date fields are silently nulled on every sync
- **Repo:** designflow-data-syncing

### BFF-L5 — `serviceMap.js` relies on fragile implicit require-order
- **Repo:** designflow-bff
- **File:** `src/serviceMap.js:2-7`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `SERVICE_MAP` reads `process.env` at require-time. It only works because `server.js` happens to `require('config/env.js')` one line earlier. Any other entry point that requires `serviceMap.js` first gets all-undefined URLs.

---

### DATA-SYNCING-L1 — Double `console.log` call logs `undefined`
- **Repo:** designflow-data-syncing
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js` (error handlers)
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `console.log(console.log(error))` — the inner `console.log` returns `undefined`, so the outer one logs `undefined`.
- **What it causes:** The error is technically printed but immediately followed by `undefined`, and the error object itself is logged without any context.

---

### DATA-SYNCING-L2 — SSE error response uses an invalid SSE field name
- **Repo:** designflow-data-syncing
- **File:** `controllers/main.controller.js:73`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** The SSE error path writes `error: ...` which is not a valid SSE field (`data:`, `event:`, `id:`, `retry:` are the valid ones).
- **What it causes:** SSE clients will never see error messages from sync jobs.

### ITEM-MASTER-L1 — `createItem` uses wrong date function (day-of-week instead of day-of-month)
- **Repo:** designflow-item-master
- **File:** `services/item_library.service.js:440-442`
- **Confidence:** Likely
- **What it is:** `date.getDay()` returns day-of-week (0–6). Almost certainly meant `getDate()` (day-of-month, 1–31). Combined with `getSeconds()+1` for uniqueness, two users creating items in the same second with the same merch group collide (triggering the infinite loop in ITEM-MASTER-H3).

---

### ITEM-MASTER-L2 — Triple CORS configuration
- **Repo:** designflow-item-master (same in tracking)
- **File:** `index.js:16, 20, 25-35`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `app.use(cors())` is registered twice plus a third manual wildcard-header middleware. One `cors()` call would do.

### ITEM-MASTER-L3 — `updateArchive` is a blind toggle
- **Repo:** designflow-item-master
- **File:** `services/art_piece.service.js:566-567`

- **What it is:** Archives by flipping `!artPiece.is_active` rather than accepting the desired state as a parameter.
- **What it causes:** Double-clicking or retrying the action un-archives the record instead of keeping it archived.

### TRACKING-L1 — `lic.model.js` AG Grid column filter uses client-supplied column name directly
- **Repo:** designflow-tracking
- **File:** `models/lic.model.js:215-231`

- **What it is:** `mapFieldToColumn` falls back to the raw client-supplied field name as a Sequelize where-clause key when the column isn't in its lookup table.
- **What it causes:** Unknown AG Grid column IDs become Sequelize column references. At worst, permits `$association.column$` Sequelize traversal syntax. At best, a 500 error.

### TRACKING-L2 — `createGroup` missing `return` after 400 response
- **Repo:** designflow-tracking
- **File:** `controllers/lic.controller.js:101-106`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** After sending a 400 response, execution falls through to the model call, which can attempt to send a second response.
- **What it causes:** `ERR_HTTP_HEADERS_SENT` error in logs on that code path.

### FRONTEND-L1 — `SanitizeHtmlPipe` does the opposite of what its name says
- **Repo:** designflow-frontend

- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** A pipe named `sanitizeHtml` calls `bypassSecurityTrustHtml`, which *disables* Angular's sanitizer rather than sanitizing. It is currently unused in any template.
- **What it causes:** Dead code today. If any developer uses this pipe on user-generated comment content, it opens a direct XSS hole (users can inject arbitrary HTML/scripts into other users' browsers).

toolUseResult.content:
# dflow PLM — Full Codebase Audit
*Reviewed: 2026-06-10 | Repos: designflow-backend, designflow-bff, designflow-data-syncing, designflow-frontend, designflow-item-master, designflow-tracking*

---

## How to read this file

Each finding has:
- **Repo** — which of the 6 repos it lives in
- **File + line** — exactly where to find it
- **Severity** — 🔴 High (security or real bug) / 🟠 Medium (likely problem or major inefficiency) / 🟡 Low (cleanup, dead code, style)
- **Confidence** — Certain / Likely / Possible
- **What it is** — plain description
- **What it causes** — real-world consequence

Findings are grouped first by severity, then by repo.

---

## Table of Contents

1. [🔴 High Severity — Security](#-high-severity--security)
2. [🔴 High Severity — Real Bugs](#-high-severity--real-bugs)
3. [🟠 Medium Severity](#-medium-severity)
4. [🟡 Low Severity](#-low-severity)
5. [Documentation Problems](#documentation-problems)
6. [Summary Table](#summary-table)

---

## 🔴 High Severity — Security

---

### BACKEND-H1 — Hardcoded master/backdoor password
- **Repo:** designflow-backend
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:1136`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `makeLoginToken` accepts any account's login if `req.passw == 'Sha14171518'`. This is a universal backdoor: anyone who knows this string can log in as any user or vendor with no other credentials.
- **What it causes:** Complete authentication bypass. Anyone — a developer, a contractor, a disgruntled employee, someone who reads the repo — can log in as any account including admins. This is the single highest-risk finding in the entire codebase.

---

### BACKEND-H2 — Live Brevo/Sendinblue API key committed in source
- **Repo:** designflow-backend
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:3612`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** A live `xkeysib-419f7a93...` email service API key is hardcoded directly in the source code and committed to the repo.
- **What it causes:** Anyone with repo access can read your email/sending logs, send bulk emails from your account, and run up charges. The key cannot be rotated without a code change and a new deploy.

---

### BACKEND-H3 — Unauthenticated paid AI proxy
- **Repo:** designflow-backend
- **File:** `routes/openrouter.router.js:35, 57`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** Neither `GET /api/openrouter/models` nor `POST /api/openrouter/chat` has any `authRole` guard. These routes are fully public.
- **What it causes:** Anyone who can reach the service can run unlimited AI conversations billed to your OpenRouter/DeepSeek account. No login, no API key, no rate limit on your end. This is an unbounded billing abuse risk.

---

### ITEM-MASTER-H1 — Hardcoded ColdLion ERP API key
- **Repo:** designflow-item-master
- **File:** `services/item_library.service.js:673`, `services/item_detail.service.js:470`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** The ColdLion ERP API key `Z21355JALT13A54L9X5` is hardcoded in plain text in two service files.
- **What it causes:** Anyone with repo access can call your external ERP system directly. The key cannot be changed without a code edit and redeploy. (This same key is also hardcoded in designflow-tracking and designflow-data-syncing — four total locations.)

---

### ITEM-MASTER-H2 — Unauthenticated full item-catalog dump
- **Repo:** designflow-item-master
- **File:** `routes/item_library.router.js:12`, `services/item_library.service.js:234-333`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `GET /lib/getApiAllItems` has no `authRole()` middleware. The service returns the entire item catalog with attachments and production-order details, unpaginated, with no login required.
- **What it causes:** Anyone who can reach the service URL gets your complete item database — no username, no password, no token needed.

---

### TRACKING-H1 — Hardcoded ColdLion API key (third copy)
- **Repo:** designflow-tracking
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:541-546`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** The ColdLion API key `Z21355JALT13A54L9X5` and endpoint `http://x5.coldlion.com/...` hardcoded in `syncPrepackAssociations`, over plain HTTP (not HTTPS).
- **What it causes:** Same as ITEM-MASTER-H1. Additionally the plain HTTP means the key travels unencrypted over the wire.

---

### TRACKING-H2 — All lead-time routes have no authentication
- **Repo:** designflow-tracking
- **File:** `routes/lead-time.router.js:6-16`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** Every other router wraps routes in `authRole([...])`. The lead-time router has none. `PUT /lead-time/design-team-times/:id`, `/licensing-times/:id`, and `/factory-times/:id` accept changes from any caller — no login required.
- **What it causes:** Anyone who can reach the service can change the master lead-time templates that drive all production needed-date calculations. Also, the PUTs pass `req.body` straight to `update()` (mass assignment — any field can be overwritten).

---

### DATA-SYNCING-H1 — Hardcoded ColdLion API key (fourth copy)
- **Repo:** designflow-data-syncing
- **File:** `models/lib.model.js:497` (and throughout sync helpers)
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** Fourth location of the same hardcoded ColdLion API key.
- **What it causes:** Same as ITEM-MASTER-H1. All four copies need to be rotated simultaneously when remediated.

---

### FRONTEND-H1 — Auth guard hangs forever on network error
- **Repo:** designflow-frontend
- **File:** `src/app/pages/auth/helpers/auth.service.ts:54-66`
- **Confidence:** Certain
- **What it is:** `verifyToken()` wraps an HTTP call in a `new Promise` whose `subscribe()` has no error handler. If the `/verifyToken` call fails for any reason (network blip, server 500), the promise never resolves.
- **What it causes:** `AuthGuard.canActivate()` hangs forever. The user gets a permanently blank/frozen screen instead of being redirected to login. Any brief server hiccup locks users out.

---

### FRONTEND-H2 — Role guards are decorative on most "admin-only" pages
- **Repo:** designflow-frontend
- **File:** `src/app/app-routing.module.ts:63-160`
- **Confidence:** Certain
